title: Meta’s New AI Pendant Set To Shock AI World - Metas AI Pendant Explained
transcript: So, Meta just confirmed it is working on a device that listens to everything you say throughout the day and uses AI to organize it for you. It's a small clip-on pendant that you wear on your clothes or around your neck, and it records your conversations, meetings, and voice notes in the background without you having to press anything. The moment people heard about it, the reaction has been split straight down the middle. Some people think this is one of the most useful AI tools ever built. Others think that this is just going to be a surveillance device that nobody has asked for. And the truth is that both sides have a point. Now, at the end of 2026 in May, The Information essentially published a report based on an internal memo from inside Meta. The memo came from Alex Himel, Meta's Vice President of Wearables, and it outlined the plan for Meta to start testing a new AI pendant within the next year, with pilots expected sometime in 2027. No public release date has been confirmed, and Meta declined to comment on the report. Now, the pendant is not the new idea that Meta came up with on their own. It's built on technology from a startup called Limitless, which Meta acquired at the end of 2025. Before the acquisition, Limitless sold its own AI device, also called the pendant, which is a small, coin-sized aluminum clip that cost $99 and could record up to 100 hours on a single charge. It connected to an app and could generate full transcripts, meeting summaries, and even link recordings to your calendars, emails, and browser history to give the AI more context about what was being discussed. So, to understand why Meta's doing this, you need to look at what is actually happening inside the company's hardware division. Meta runs something called Reality Labs, which is the part of the company responsible for all of its hardware products. From the Quest headsets to the Ray-Ban smart glasses, Reality Labs has been losing billions of dollars for years. The pressure on Zuckerberg to show that hardware can actually be a serious business for Meta is enormous, and the pendant is one piece of a wider plan to fix that. The memo also revealed that Meta is planning to release up to four more models of the Ray-Ban glasses before the end of 2026, and it is introducing something called wearables for work, a business-focused version of its wearable products aimed at enterprise customers. The pendant fits into that same strategy. The goal isn't just to sell hardware, it is to get more people using Meta's AI models directly through the devices they wear and convert those users into paying subscribers for Meta's AI services. Now, you have to wonder, why does this matter? Because right now we are moving into a different era of AI that most people don't understand. And that era that we're moving into is ambient AI. And this is one of the most contested spaces in all of consumer tech right now. The core idea is essentially simple. Instead of pulling out your phone to take notes, to take a message, or ask an AI something, the device just runs in the background and captures everything. They're when you need it, and it's always there. And if you need it, you can already have the full context about what happened, who said what, and when. And that is genuinely useful. Anyone who has ever sat through a two-hour meeting and then struggled to remember key decisions three days later can see that appeal. Meta's Ray-Ban glasses already do something similar with the cameras. The pendant takes that further because the microphone is always on. But here's where it gets complicated. Meta is not exactly the company that users trust most with their personal data. I mean, the Ray-Ban glasses have already attracted a class-action lawsuit alleging that footage captured by the glasses was reviewed by contractors without proper disclosure. There's ongoing investigations by the UK Commissioner's office and Kenya's office of data protection commissioner, and none of that footage included audio. A device that records everything you say is a much bigger step. The reaction to this announcement has been predictable but not unreasonable. Tech enthusiasts who already use AI tools daily are excited. They want something that integrates into their workflow without friction. And a pendant that records meetings and generate summaries automatically sounds like a productivity win. But a large group of people are comfortable with the core concept. Research from the ambient wearable market found that 58% of non-purchasers in 2025 cited always-on listening as the primary reason for not buying a device like this. And that number isn't small. More than half the people who looked at the category and decided to pass made the decision specifically because the microphone never turns off. And this is not a new problem. Previous AI wearables have tried and failed in exactly this space. Humane's AI Pin was one of the most anticipated AI hardware products in years. [music] It launched to brutal reviews and effectively collapsed. The Friend AI necklace, which was marketed as a companion device that listens to your environment, had its promotional posters physically vandalized in New York. The market for always-on AI wearables for a while is very difficult to crack. Now, we have to think about what happens next. There are two main questions hanging over this product. The first is whether Meta can make the device actually useful enough so that people choose to wear every day. The original Limitless Pendant actually had some fans, but it was a niche product and Limitless shut down production after Meta bought the company. Existing users were given 1 year of continued support and then the product was discontinued across the globe. Now, Meta will need to rebuild customer trust in the device from scratch. Now, the second question is regulatory. The EU is already scrutinizing Meta's glasses under GDPR and the Digital Markets Act. A pendant that records conversations is likely to face even tighter scrutiny and particularly in Europe where the rules around recording third parties without their consent are strict. Under GDPR Article 13, recording speech from people who have not consented to being recorded is not a gray area. It is a serious legal issue. Some vendors in this space have responded by adding audible consent tones and physical mute buttons with LED indicators. But there is no indication of what Meta's approach will look like. Amazon actually recently launched a similar device called B, a clip-on wearable with continuous a built in. But that product also triggered immediate privacy criticism, and Meta will be watching how B performs before it goes too far down the road with its own version. Now, Meta is making a serious bet on wearable AI, and the pendant is the most ambitious part of the bet. The technology already exists. The The Limitless device already proved the core concept to works at a basic level, and the problem has never been whether you can build the device. The problem is whether people will actually want to wear it, and whether the people around them will accept being recorded every time they enter the room. If Meta gets the privacy architecture right, prices it competitively, and can tie it to a useful AI experience with other platforms, it has a real shot at being the first company to make AI wearables mainstream. But, if it gets the privacy story wrong, it will become the next example of an AI hardware product that sounded good on paper, and then collapsed when it met the real world. The testing begins in 2027, and that's when we're going to start to find out which version of the story we're actually living in. Now, if you guys want my own personal opinion on where I think this device will land, I think this device is going to have to be very careful with how they market themselves because, as I've already discussed several times in this video, Meta just simply don't have the right privacy when it comes to branding, and considering what they did with people's data in the past, [music] nobody is really going to trust this. I think that if Meta are going to be successful with this, [music] the way that they should roll this out is probably first for enterprise use cases, for example, such as meetings and business deals. That kind of environment where perhaps you already have a note-taker, considering that those markets already growing organically, I think that that genuinely makes sense. Maybe Meta needs to just grab a foothold there, and then from grabbing a foothold there, then they can potentially go into other areas that actually make sense. But, just throwing it down into consumer markets, I can see this immediately getting a negative reaction. Another thing that, you know, a lot of people aren't realizing is that Meta glasses are so popular now that a lot of people actually started to despise them because they often record people without their consent, which is once again a little bit of a grey area when it comes to privacy. So, Meta's going to have to carefully manage the brand image of this device to prevent it from being something that just becomes another piece of spyware that people are afraid of rather than a piece of technology that people will actually want to use. And so, I'd love to know your thoughts about this device, if you would actually get one, if you're actually using any AI devices right now, I would love to know. I actually don't use any AI devices, but let me know what you guys think.
